title: The Fair Shake
story:
You know why I started Vanguard? Because ordinary people weren't getting a fair shake.

Wall Street was taking their money through high fees, complex products, and empty promises. Smart people in expensive suits, taking advantage of teachers and nurses and factory workers who just wanted to retire someday.

That made me angry. It still makes my blood boil.

I could have played the game. Made more money. Been popular in the industry. But I couldn't sleep at night knowing I was part of the problem.

So I built something different. A company owned by its fund shareholders. No outside investors to please. Just ordinary people, getting a fair shake.

They called me naive. They called me a troublemaker. They said it would never work.

Fifty years later, Vanguard manages trillions. Turns out, doing the right thing was also good business.

When you're facing an ethical choice, ask yourself: Can I look in the mirror? Can I explain this to my grandchildren? Is this who I want to be?

The right thing and the easy thing are rarely the same. That's how you know the difference.
followup_questions: What's the ethical question you're wrestling with?
What would you tell a friend in this situation?
Can you look in the mirror with this decision?
